1.description: Information inertia for the risk-free and risky portfolio. The graph shows the optimal asset demand as a function of the signal for an MEU investor (black solid line), for an SEU investor who underestimates the informativeness of the signal (ρa, blue-dashed circle line), for an SEU investor who correctly estimates the informativeness of the signal (ρ̂, purple dotted-plus line), for an SEU investor who overestimates the informativeness of the signal (ρb, red chain-dotted square line), and for an SEU investor who does not receive the signal (green dotted-cross line). There is a range of signals for which a risky portfolio and the risk-free portfolio do not react to signals that convey worse news than expected. The parameters are: unconditional asset meand¯=100, asset pricep=95, unconditional asset varianceσd2=20, risk aversionγ=1, and asset correlations that satisfyρaσd=2,ρ̂σd=3, andρbσd=4. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Information inertia for the risky portfolio—The mechanism. The graphs show investors' perceived (log of the) conditional Sharpe ratio and volatility as a function of a signal. The blue dashed-circle line represents an SEU investor who underestimates the informativeness of the signal (ρa), the purple dotted-lines represent SEU investors with different beliefsρ∈(ρa,ρb), the red chain-dotted square line represents an SEU investor who overestimates the informativeness of the signal (ρb), and the black solid line represents the MEU investor. There is a range of signals for which the Sharpe ratio and the volatility change at the same rate, leading to no change in the demand for the asset and thus local information inertia for risky portfolios. The parameters are: unconditional asset meand¯=100, asset pricep=95, unconditional asset varianceσd2=20, risk aversionγ=1, and asset correlations that satisfyρaσd=2andρbσd=4. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Informational inefficiency. The graph shows the equilibrium price of the market portfolio as a function of a signal about the future aggregate dividend. The blue dashed-circle line represents an economy with an SEU-RI who underestimates the informativeness of the signal, the purple dotted-plus line represents an economy with a SEU-RI who correctly estimates the informativeness of the signal, the red chain-dotted square line represents an economy with an SEU-RI who overestimates the informativeness of the signal, and the black solid line represents an economy with an MEU-RI. There is a range of signals for which the equilibrium price does not react (black solid line) to changes in the signal, leading to informational inefficiency. The parameters are: unconditional dividend meand¯=100, unconditional dividend volatilityσd=5, risk aversionγ=1, and asset correlationsρa=0.2,ρ̂=0.4, andρb=0.6. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Informational inefficiency—Quantitative importance. The graph shows the equilibrium price as a function of a signal with average informativeness about dividend growthρ̂2=16%and ambiguityα=0.99. The unconditional risk premium ranges from2.5%to10%. There is a range of DP ratios for which the equilibrium price does not depend on the DP ratio, in which case the dividend information conveyed by the DP ratio is not efficiently incorporated into the price. The table shows the probability of obtaining informational inefficiency conditional on a bad news surprise for different ambiguity(α), average informativeness (ρ̂2), and unconditional risk premiums (γσd2). The severity of this informational inefficiency increases in ambiguity and risk. The economy consists of an ambiguity-averse RI with CRRA utility, the expected rate of dividend growth is2%and dividend growth volatility is12%. The DP ratio is always statistically significant because the confidence interval that represents ambiguity never contains zero, soρa>0for allα∈{0.9,0.95,0.99}. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: News momentum. Both graphs show the slope of a news momentum regression as a function of the stock market risk premium. The left graph shows that excess stock market returns underreact to news about the market portfolio (e.g., macroeconomic news) and that the resulting positive slope coefficient increases in the stock market risk premiumγσd2, ambiguityα, and average informativeness of the signalρ̂. The right graph shows that risk-adjusted stock returns underreact to news about a firm (e.g., earnings news) and that the resulting positive slope coefficient increases in the stock market risk premiumγσd2and is nonmonotonic inω, which captures how much of the positive correlation between the firm's dividend and the signal is due to the systematic cash flow component. The cash flow beta of the asset shown isβ1=1.5and the market explains25%of the variation in the asset returns. Similar to the left graph, the slope in the right graph also increases in ambiguity and the average informativeness of the signal. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Robustness. The left graph shows the equilibrium price as a function of the signal, and the right graph shows the slope coefficients from a regression of excess returns on a constant and the signal. The blue dashed-circle line and black solid line verify that there is a range of signals for which the equilibrium price does not react in the CRRA/log-normal model and the CARA/normal model, respectively. Deviating from these models by considering (i) different joint distributions of asset payoffs and signals, (ii) economies populated with ambiguity-averse and ambiguity-neutral investors, and (iii) other preference models that allow for a distinction between ambiguity and ambiguity aversion leads to low sensitivity to news instead of information inertia. The economic significance of the news momentum drift is of similar magnitude in all cases. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
